long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Summary: The project aims to increase the income of 7,800 cotton producer families through supporting them to form 105 community-based producer groups to receive training in good agricultural, environmental, social and business practices. Source Trust (ST) in partnership with the Great Lakes Cotton Company Ltd, (GLCC) will support farmers to form groups through 15 Agribusiness Centres in 3 districts which are producing 50% of Malawis cotton. The project aims to increase productivity and producer income by 35% over three years while at the same time reducing the negative impacts of cotton production on the environment. An expert in water management will undertake a study against the criteria for water management included in the Cotton Made in Africa (CmiA) and Better Cotton Initiative (BCI) standards. The cotton produced will feed into the Sainsbury garment supply chain with the manufacture of final products in Bangladesh. 4,000 women in the project area will be supported to grow cassava and sweet potato to improve food security whilst improving their leadership skills. 4,500 in-school youth will be provided with training in cotton production on plots of cotton with the proceeds going to benefit the schools to help stem the flow of youth moving away from agricultural production. Full grant commitment: GBP 250 thousand.
channel_name: Donor country-based NGO

long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): NULL. Summary: Rwanda has many young orphaned heads of households as a result of genocide and HIV/AIDS. World Jewish Relief and its local NGO partner, Uyisenga Ni Imanzi, began in 2012 to build the business skills and technical and organisational capacity of young orphaned men and women farming fruits and vegetables. The project will scale up to increase incomes by 60% of 1,487 people (60% women) in three districts, upgrade their associations to formal cooperatives, improve their agronomic techniques and product mix, based on business analysis from the pilot, and link them to markets. 60 registered cooperatives will graduate to viability and vulnerable individuals will gain confidence and a livelihood. The government of the UK (DfID) funded 50 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant commitment: GBP 365.994 thousand.
channel_name: Donor country-based NGO

long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Summary: People living in the Itezhitezhi district of Zambia face short periods of food surpluses followed by months of food shortages because they are unable to preserve the surplus food or sell it at a profit when it is available. Many depend on forest products such as wild mushrooms or roots and tubers during periods of shortage but these are being threatened by logging and woodland clearance for cultivation. In the neighbouring district of Choma a food processing and marketing project has increased household incomes, nutrition and self-confidence. This project aims to gather the information necessary to introduce a similar project in Itezhitezhi. The government of the UK (DfID) funded 60 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant commitment: GBP 9.9 thousand.
channel_name: Donor country-based NGO

long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): Lack of voice, influence, agency. Summary: After long civil war, farmers in the Casamance, Senegal, are developing the economy and have several unusual wild fruits to sell which qualify as so-called superfoods. This project helps 2,200 farmers to organise a marketing association, set up collection centres and care better for their land and wild forests. Around 880 women farmers will be directly supported to meet, learn new skills and to ensure their active representation in the new organisations. Two Senegalese social enterprises are contributing to the project and provide the link to growing European markets. A UK Fair Trade juice pioneer will build a Fair Trade brand and means of distribution for these unique products. The government of the UK (DfID) funded 50 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant commitment: GBP 625.489 thousand.
channel_name: Donor country-based NGO

short_description: SOLIDARIDAD EASTERN AND CENTRAL AFRICA EXPERTISE CENTRE (SECACEC)
long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): Lack of voice, influence, agency. Summary: Coffee is Ethiopias greatest export earner. In spite of many farmers being organized in Fairtrade cooperatives, coffee yields, quality and incomes remain low. Womens membership is minimal and they do not see the income from their labour in coffee. This project addresses the root causes of persistent barriers to improve coffee production and income for men and women, and diversification of income from dairy through practical training. Working with the largest cooperative union in Ethiopia, the project will build their capacity to service their members to improve quality and combat climate change, distribute coffee seedlings, diversify income and increase womens participation. The government of the UK (DfID) funded 50 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant commitment: GBP 835.646 thousand.
channel_name: Networks
project_title: Solidaridad Eastern and Central Africa Expertise Centre (SECACEC)

long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): NULL. Summary: The project will respond to growing urban demand in Tanzania for processed potato products by increasing the supply of good quality potatoes, facilitating investment in processing, and building collaboration in the supply chain so that each part upgrades with confidence and coordination. The project will form business groups of 20,000 smallholder farmers, 1,500 small retailers and 13,500 vendors (30% women) of potato products around lead firms. Their incomes will double over four years. Project funds will build capacity, remove barriers to efficiency and profitability, provide co-finance and facilitation and manage MEL. Business groups will co-invest because of growing supply and demand. The government of the UK (DfID) funded 50 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant commitment: GBP 1718.139 thousand.
channel_name: Developing country-based NGO

long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): Education, Health, Trade, enterprise and employment. Summary: Poor farmers in northern Malawi live on what they can grow, bartering or selling any surplus food in local markets to buy clothes and essentials leaving no cash for healthcare or secondary education. Many farmers have a small amount of land available and MF and NFT are working with them to introduce suitable cash crops with a guaranteed market (e.g. paprika). Irrigated land can double the yields of these crops and protect staple foods. However, many past schemes to introduce irrigation have failed due to technical flaws, inappropriate design and lack of training. This project aims to identify schemes which can be rehabilitated, the support needs of farmers and a business case for investors. The government of the UK (DfID) funded 60 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant commitment: GBP 9.1 thousand.
channel_name: Donor country-based NGO

long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): NULL. Summary: Certified fruits and nuts are an important source of income for thousands of farmers in Burkina Faso and neighbouring countries, also creating steady jobs in transport, distribution, and processing for hundreds of workers, mostly women, in small enterprises and cooperatives.  Fullwell Mill and Gebana are two social enterprises with a vision of helping more than 2,000 of these farmers and entrepreneurs to move up the value chain. The project will help them to plant more cashew trees, improve farming and yields, do more primary processing, reduce costs and earn more, whatever their role.  The project will also test alternative cashew products and how to make safe energy from cashew nut waste. The government of the UK (DfID) funded 50 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant commitment: GBP 444.557 thousand.

long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): NULL. Summary: Cassava is a staple food crop in Sierra Leone but the Northern Province, struggling to recover from the effects of the protected civil war and the Ebola crisis, imports expensive cassava from other parts of the country. Local cassava farmers and processors, unable to access the services and support they need to increase yields and produce reasonably priced cassava products, are unable to fill this gap in the market. This project aims to enable cassava farmers and processors to form commercially viable cooperatives able to access the training, machinery and financial support needed to bring high quality, locally sourced cassava products to market and, as they grow, create local jobs. The government of the UK (DfID) funded 50 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant commitment: GBP 432.833 thousand.
channel_name: Donor country-based NGO

long_description: Primary goal (Comic Relief): Trade, enterprise and employment. Related issues (Comic Relief): Lack of voice, influence, agency. Summary: Dairy production in and around Arusha, Tanzania, has good potential, and demand is growing especially for high value butter, cheese and fruit yoghurt. Twenty womens dairy groups with nearly 2,000 members are in a strong position to benefit from this, but need alot of help to organise themselves, to brand and distribute their products, collect and process milk regularly, upgrade the factory, and work together to be more efficient. Selling more milk means earning more for their households directly. Promoting a joint venture will add to their negotiation power for inputs like fodder, veterinary services, and transport and to mobilise more back up from Government to help care for their cows The government of the UK (DfID) funded 50 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant commitment: GBP 1875.259 thousand.

long_description: Programme: Seed Award in Science. Project: Integrated Nucleic Acid Microsensor for On-Site Detection of Infectious Animal Diseases. Grantee location: UK. Description: Despite the advancement of diagnostic technologies targeting nucleic acids (NA), there are essentially no portable, disposable and integrated solutions for testing of infectious diseases at the point-of-care (PoC). This is unfortunately the case for all infectious diseases affecting animals, humans and even plants. To address this, we will develop a new class of NA microsensor namely, Silicon-Paper integrated Diagnostics or SPiDx. This new technology will combine the versatility of paper-based microfluidics and performance of silicon MEMS (microelectromechanical systems) on the same platform for the first time. SPiDx will be capable of integrated thermal amplification and electrochemical detection of NA at the PoC, while supporting multiplexed analysis. To enable ultra-low-cost devices, the entire SPiDx platform will be produced outside the cleanroom, overcoming the main barrier for Si MEMS technologies (i.e. high cost of manufacturing).In this project, we will target diseases that affect food animals, as a model disease, we will focus on paratuberculosis. Paratuberculosis is a fatal, chronic wasting disease affecting ruminant animals with significant economic consequences. Mycobacterium avium subspecies paratuberculosis (MAP), the causative agent responsible for paratuberculosis may also be associated with Crohn's disease in humans. These factors combined make MAP an important target for detection.

short_description: A CASE FOR INVESTMENT IN ZOONOTIC TUBERCULOSIS
long_description: Programme: Seed Award in Science. Description: Zoonotic tuberculosis (zTB) is a form of tuberculosis (TB) disease in humans caused by infection with Mycobacterium bovis , the main cause of TB in cattle . The World Health Organization estimates that there are 150,000 new zTB cases annually worldwide, but this is an underestimate because zTB is clinically indistinguishable from other forms of TB and the majority of cases occur in rural populations with limited access to healthcare. zTB has been overlooked because of its relatively small contribution to the global TB burden. However, zTB is now receiving attention because of the 2035 target for global TB elimination. zTB was explicitly included in the Global Plan to End TB 2016-2020. In 2017, the &lsquo,Roadmap for Zoonotic Tuberculosis was published. Currently, there is a lack of data to support investment in zTB. An exception is the UK, where routinely-collected data on TB cases are strain-typed for both humans and cattle. Using the rich UK data, I aim to determine: i) the human infection risk associated with infected cattle, ii) the population-level correlation between human and cattle cases. Achieving these goals will enable accurate estimates of the global zTB burden and provide evidence to support UK TB policy.
channel_name: University, college or other teaching institution, research institute or think-tank
project_title: A Case for Investment in Zoonotic tuberculosis

long_description: Stichting DOEN programme: Groen . Agsol LimitedDescription: Agsol Limited produceert en verkoopt maalmachines (agro-processing machines) op zonne-energie voor gemeenschappen die niet aangesloten zijn op het algemene elektriciteitsnetwerk. Deze machines verwerken de belangrijkste voedingsmiddelen, zoals gierst, cassave, graan, rijst en mais, tot eetbare voedingsproducten. Deze maalmachines vervangen huidige alternatieven die op diesel draaien en kunnen bijdragen aan meer economische activiteit in rurale gebieden. Financiering van DOEN stelt Agsol in staat producten te verbeteren en 570 maalmachines te verkopen binnen 2 jaar waarmee meer dan 50.000[1] boeren toegang krijgen tot deze diensten.

long_description: Dairy production in and around Arusha, Tanzania, has good potential, and demand is growing especially for high value butter, cheese and fruit yoghurt. Twenty womens' dairy groups with nearly 2,000 members are in a strong position to benefit from this, but need alot of help to organise themselves, to brand and distribute their products, collect and process milk regularly, upgrade the factory, and work together to be more efficient. Selling more milk means earning more for their households directly. Promoting a joint venture will add to their negotiation power for inputs like fodder, veterinary services, and transport and to mobilise more back up from Government to help care for their cows Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office funded 21 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. short_description: OROMIA COFFEE AND DAIRY DEVELOPMENT PROJECT (OCDDP): PROMOTING GENDER AND ENTREP
long_description: Coffee is Ethiopia?s greatest export earner. In spite of many farmers being organized in Fairtrade cooperatives, coffee yields, quality and incomes remain low. Women?s membership is minimal and they do not see the income from their labour in coffee. This project addresses the root causes of persistent barriers to improve coffee production and income for men and women, and diversification of income from dairy through practical training. Working with the largest cooperative union in Ethiopia, the project will build their capacity to service their members to improve quality and combat climate change, distribute coffee seedlings, diversify income and increase women?s participation.

long_description: After long civil war, farmers in the Casamance, Senegal, are developing the economy and have several unusual wild fruits to sell which qualify as so-called ?superfoods?. This project helps 2,200 farmers to organise a marketing association, set up collection centres and care better for their land and wild forests. Around 880 women farmers will be directly supported to meet, learn new skills and to ensure their active representation in the new organisations. Two Senegalese social enterprises are contributing to the project and provide the link to growing European markets. A UK Fair Trade juice pioneer will build a Fair Trade brand and means of distribution for these unique products. Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office funded 50 percent of this grant - this share is excluded to avoid double counting in the DAC statistics. Full grant amount: GBP 72954

short_description: HARNESSING THE POWER OF PLANT ROOT SYSTEMS
long_description: Programme: Nature Solutions. Description: Every year, plants capture hundreds of billions of tons of CO2 through photosynthesis and then funnel large amounts of carbon into the soil. This project leverages work in genetics, genomics and biochemistry to enhance this carbon-storing ability of plant roots so that they can capture excess atmospheric carbon. By focusing on prevalent crop plants—which can be readily scaled up within the existing agriculture infrastructure — this project aims to mitigate the effects of climate change, develop more resilient plants and improve soil health.

short_description: SUPPORTING AND ENHANCING RESILIENT AND VIABLE EMPLOYMENT OPPORTUNITIES SERVE
long_description: Programme: Young Africa Works Country Programs. Sub-programme: Young Africa Works Rwanda. Description: SERVE: Supporting and Enhancing Resilient and Viable Employment Opportunities, CARE International, along with consortium partners Duharanire Amajyambere Y'Icyaro (DUHAMIC-ADRI), Pro-femme Twese Hamwe (PFTH), Association of Micro Finance Institutions Rwanda (AMIR), and Urwego Bank, present SERVE, an agricultural enterprise and financial inclusion intervention delivered over five years, with the objective,to build a resilient, sustainable, gender,equitable and inclusive entrepreneurial environment that increases dignified and fulfilling work opportunities for predominantly female youth in agricultural value chains in 10 districts across Rwanda. This will be brought about by two key changes. Firstly, inclusive sustainable growth for youth-led agricultural Micro and Small Enterprises (MSEs) will be brought about by strengthening the growth levers of productivity, access to finance, entrepreneurship and market linkages in the green bean, chili pepper, tomato and poultry value chains (Outcome 1). Secondly, an enhanced policy and social norms environment will encourage and enable key stakeholders to deliver a more equitable, inclusive and responsive agricultural sector, thus reducing key policy and social barriers to entry for youth, especially female youth, enabling their participation and valuing their contributions as economic and agricultural actors (Outcome 2). Beyond the consortium partners, several different types of strategic alliances will be harnessed in the project to achieve these two outcomes. Key partnerships to highlight include national government ministries such as the Ministry of Agriculture and Animal Resources (MINAGRI), the Ministry of Trade and Industry (MINICOM), and the Ministry of Finance and Economic Planning (MINECOFIN). Key civil society partners include the Chamber of Youth and Associations, and the Rwandan Youth in Agriculture Forum (RYAF). Each partner will have a specific role in the project and will bring value-add with their unique experience and expertise. Collaborating with the private sector is a central aspect of SERVE. Financial service providers (FSPs), including microfinance institutions, banks and insurance providers, will work in close collaboration with CARE to develop tailormade formal financial products/services for participants. This work will also focus on accessible digital solutions in order to accelerate reach and systematically address the gendered digital divide which blocks female, especially rural, youth from accessing formal financial services. Furthermore, CARE will collaborate with the private sector for the entrepreneurship components, including linking female youth with mentors, potential buyers and regulators. SERVE will target 100,000 predominantly female youth (PFY) (at least 70% female) engaged in or with the potential to engage in agricultural businesses and smallholder farming, also know
channel_name: International NGO
project_title: Supporting and Enhancing Resilient and Viable Employment Opportunities SERVE

short_description: 2022 - SCALING COMMERCIALIZATION OF DROUGHT TOLERANT CROPS (DTC) TECHNOLOGIES TO SECURE DIGNIFIED AND FULFILLING WORK FOR 120,000 YOUTH IN RURAL KENYA
long_description: Programme: Young Africa Works Country Programs. Sub-programme: Young Africa Works Kenya. Description: The commercialization of DTC technologies aligns well the the Mastercard Foundation's country strategy under agriculture, of strengthened, inclusive , resilient and sustainable market ecosystems with a key focus of enabling opportunities for young people to actively and gainfully be engaged along the various nodes of the value chain hence contributing to various outcomes including enhanced performance of Ag businesses particularly those owned by young women and men, increased entrepreneurship opportunities for young people, within Ag-value chains , enhanced control of Ag-resources by young men and women and others.Africa Harvest and other partners, such as ICRISAT,KALRO, Egerton University and County governments, have previously implemented projects in Arid and Semi-Arid lands (ASALs) of Kenya that developed, validated and promoted Drought Tolerant Crop (DTC) technologies which underpin the food, nutritional and income security of many people inhabiting,, the semi-arid agro ecologies . The proposed project will scale the use of DTC technologies to catalyse the creation of 120,000 dignified, sustainable and fulfilling work for youth (70% young women & 30% young men. With the increasing negative effects of climate change to farm families manifested through prolonged periods of drought, erratic rainfall patterns and pests and diseases, the proposed project ,will,,build resilience of food systems to withstand shocks of an extended or unexpected drought as a result of climate change. The targets sets above, will be achieved through activities designed around 3 main components namely, (1) Production and marketing of improved DTC seeds for 3 cereals and 3 legumes (2) Cereal and legume grain production, productivity, and quality enhancement and (3)Value addition, product development market, and finance access. ,Overall enterprise development will be a cross-cutting issue in the 3 components of the project for supported enterprises to create more employment, increase income, and to enhance sustainability. These core components will be supported by a number of enablers that include, IT-based integration for E-learning and digital platforms and advisories, Financial inclusion, Enterprise development and training, Communication and Knowledge management, Monitoring, evaluation, and learning (MEL), Public-Private Partnerships and policy. To achieve the 70% gender target, the project,will be a deliberate, consultative, and inclusive approach to mobilizing Young women to join the initiative and form common interest groups for collective effort and sharing of duties.,A sound gender framework is critical to enabling the project to take into perspective the concerns and specific needs of intended beneficiaries. The team proposes adopting the feminist approach to ensure that women and youth from diverse backgrounds are not only targeted but also included in the cour
channel_name: Developing country-based NGO
project_title: 2022 - Scaling Commercialization of Drought Tolerant Crops (DTC) Technologies to Secure Dignified and Fulfilling Work for 120,000 Youth in Rural Kenya

short_description: 2022 REPOSITIONING AGRICULTURE AND TECHNICAL VOCATIONAL EDUCATION AND TRAINING (TVET) AS CAREER OPPORTUNITIES FOR YOUNG GHANAIANS ESPECIALLY WOMEN
long_description:
Programme: Young Africa Works Country Programs. Sub-programme: Young Africa Works Ghana. Description: Young Africa Works is the Mastercard Foundation's strategy aimed at enabling 30 million young Africans (70% young women) access dignified and fulfilling work. It is envisaged that Africa will be home to the world's largest workforce, with 375 million young people entering the job market by 2030.,In Ghana, the Foundation's portfolio is designed to empower and transition young Ghanaians to dignified and fulfilling jobs by:
    Focusing on youth –,Enabling 3 million Ghanaian young people to access dignified and fulfilling work by 2030,Empowering young women –,Assisting 70% women across initiatives to deal with the inequity in gender-based barriers such as skills development, access to land and affordable credit.,Creating long-term, systemic change at scale –,Partnering with Africa-based organizations to embed long-term, transformational change that stimulates resilience to economic shocks
This proposal showcases the strategic program we will use to facilitate these 3 prospects for youth in Ghana. Our program will seek to tackle fundamental perceptions regarding the unattractiveness of the agriculture and Technical Vocational Education and Training sectors (TVET) which have been identified as sectors of high employment opportunities, raise awareness of young people especially young women around policies in the sectors that enable them access the opportunities, train the youth,,to in the skills needed to meet those demands in the sectors and match them to work opportunities in the Agriculture, AG-adjacent and TVET sectors.,GHANA GROWS is a 3-year multi-faceted perception change program aimed at catalyzing young people's misconceptions about the Agriculture, Agricultural TVET (ATVET) and AG-adjacent sectors' key work opportunities, relative to the offerings of other sectors. The program,will provide appropriate through-the-line (TTL) awareness creation tools, career guidance and counseling platforms, policy training and engagement forums, work readiness and soft skills as well as job linkage opportunities to youth between the ages of 15 -35, particularly women and differently abled youth at the grassroots/informal and formal sectors,,it will,provide the opportunities for the targeted youth to gain the required employability skills to succeed in transitioning to jobs,within the two identified sectors,thereby aligning with the Foundation's Charitable purpose,of,programs and services to help children and youth develop the skills necessary to succeed in a diverse and global work force.,All activities will be delivered at no cost to the youth participants.,Ghana Grows,will be unique for its youth agency because it is informed,by young people, with young people and for young people, especially young women.Ghana Grows (GG) will be implemented by a consortium made up of a lead aggregator, a sub-implementer and service providers.,
channel_name: Developing country-based NGO
project_title: 2022 Repositioning Agriculture and Technical Vocational Education and Training (TVET) as career opportunities for young Ghanaians especially women

short_description: 2023 - BUILDING AN ENABLING ENVIRONMENT FOR ENTREPRENEURSHIP AND MSME GROWTH IN THE AGRICULTURE, TOURISM & HOSPITALITY VALUE CHAINS
long_description:
MSMEs are critical in driving inclusive growth and employment for the vulnerable poor and young people, especially women in Rwanda. Agriculture and Tourism &, Hospitality (T&,H), which are key productive/growth sectors for MSMEs in Rwanda, have the potential to employ many young women and men, the majority of whom are poor and live in rural areas. However, these sectors continue to face multiple challenges, including low business management capabilities and limited access to appropriate financing, and their potential needs to be harnessed to open opportunities for dignified and fulfilling work for young women and men. Based on the UNDP Multidimensional Poverty Index 2022, It's estimated that 48.8 percent of the population in Rwanda is multidimensionally poor while an additional 22.7 percent is classified as vulnerable to multidimensional poverty. Almost 30% of the Rwandan population is below the age of 30 and 51.5% of Rwandans are women. Therefore, this program is designed with population structure in mind. Through access to appropriate financing solutions and skills development through BDS provision in the Terimbere shared services and local BDS service providers, this target group will be reached and served. In turn, this will lead to solving Rwanda's key employment challenges and relieve the poverty of the targeted marginalized and financially disadvantaged youth and women.We anticipate unlocking and enabling solutions and avenues beyond the Foundation project using the market systems approach.

The proposed program seeks to address identified constraints to SME Financing and their root causes and to contribute to the Foundation's intended intermediate outcomes through:

  • Enabled and better-coordinated ecosystem to foster entrepreneurship among young women and men,
  • Improved access to domestic, regional, and international markets by SMEs,
  • Strengthening access to tailored business support services to improve businesses' growth, survival, governance, and efficiency across MSMEs within the Agriculture and T&,H sectors.
  • Increased MSMEs' growth and resilience through appropriate BDS and access to tailored and innovative finance.
  • Strengthened and more efficient agriculture market linkages that improve profitability.
  • Increased access to finance for women investments in the selected value chains.
  • Increased diversification by the private sector and quality of Tourism experiences and offerings to create a more competitive, resilient, innovative, and vibrant sector.
  • Digital platforms (Increased Investments, transactions, innovation, and access to information)
The program will leverage AFR's expertise in Market Systems Development (MSD) approach and market facilitation to ensure ecosystem transformation and scale for sustainable impact on youth livelihood. It is part of a tripartite consortium between AFR, ESPartners, and BPN Rwanda, where AFR plays the consortium lead role in line with its market catalyst role, and it will build on key lessons learnt from existing partnership with the foundation and scale what was achieved, turn learnings into actions for the next generations financial services. AFR intends to implement this program via five interventions: (a) Implement a commercial SME Finance Readiness Support/ Terimbere Shared Services Village (SSV) as means of relieving poverty, (b) Improving policy, legal and regulatory environment and norms to foster poverty eradication , (c) Supporting Research, data analytics, and knowledge to catalyse innovation and support mechanism for poverty relief (d) Strengthening the capacity of Business Development Services Providers (BDSPs) to effectively serve MSMEs in financially disadvantaged communities an
channel_name: Developing country-based NGO
project_title: 2023 - Building an enabling environment for entrepreneurship and MSME growth in the Agriculture, Tourism & Hospitality value chains

short_description: 2023 AGRICULTURE-FOCUSED DIGNIFIED EMPLOYMENT FOR YOUTH IN ETHIOPIA (ADEY)
long_description: Ethiopia's national unemployment rate has increased from 4.5% to 8% in the last decade. Female and disadvantaged youth face disproportionate political, structural, and socio-cultural barriers to employment. Agriculture in Ethiopia is the mainstay for 85% of the population contributing to 33% of GDP and 76% of exports. It employs 66% of the population, but youth seem not to consider agriculture an attractive opportunity<sup>[1]</sup>. Youth have limited hard and soft skills, and mindsets to engage in agriculture and agro-processing activities. In addition, access to finance, markets, and business and policy ecosystems are not tailored to the needs of youth. Sparse engagement of the private sector in agriculture and agro-processing sectors also hinders their employment opportunities. Cognizant of these facts, the ATI and Mercy Corps have designed the Agriculture-focused Dignified Employment for Youth in Ethiopia (ADEY) program to address the constraints and increase the participation of youth in the agriculture sector. Considerable employment opportunities have been created for youth through ATI and Mercy Corps innovative projects over the last 12 years. ATI works with more than 3 million farmers (540,300 women and youth) engaged in the Agricultural Commercialization Clusters (ACC) and digital agriculture programs designed to improve farmers' productivity while supporting actors along the value chain. Mercy Corps, with others, has successfully reached over 16 million farmers through innovative initiatives in agriculture, and promoted inclusive market systems development and system change interventions, engaging with 2,400 enterprises to improve the livelihoods of millions over the last five years.   Taking ATI's and Mercy Corps' reputable experience, this program is designed to create work opportunities in Amhara, Oromia, SNNP, Sidama, Southwest, Somali, Gambella, and Afar regions for 1 million youth. The program focuses on agriculture and MSMEs to strengthen access to input/output markets and finance, improve the competitiveness of youth-owned MSMEs through capacity-building, enhance the skills and mindsets of youth via skill development and create an enabling environment through policy interventions.    The interventions will be implemented in selected commodities and on and off-farm value chains that have the largest potential for job creation and improvement of livelihoods. The commodities prioritized for the program include Wheat, Soyabean, Tomato, Onion, Avocado, Banana, Poultry, Dairy, and Apiculture. These commodities are produced by a significant portion of the population with demonstrated potential to increase income, create jobs, and have strong value-addition, agro-processing and export potential. The program plans to take an agile approach and will continuously evaluate potential and success and maintain an open approach to adopt commodities that present the highest potential. In addition, the program also focuses on supporting MSMEs that provide required inputs such as livestock feed, improved seed, agrochemicals, agri-lime, biofertilizers, vermicompost, and technologies that support production such as mechanization, irrigation, artificial insemination, fattening, and other post-harvest technologies. This program is designed to accelerate job creation by ATI and Mercy Corps by enhancing the support provided to entrepreneurs and wage-employment seekers in parallel with their existing programs. This will be achieved through improving access to technologies and good practices, establishing, and strengthening youth-owned MSMEs, supporting innovative, profitable, and digital businesses appropriate for youth, improving access to inputs, credit, and output markets, and thereby enhancing the knowledge, skill, and mindsets of youth to remain competitive in the commodity and labor markets for employment, entrepreneurship and overall development in agriculture.   While implementing this program, a total of 1.3 million youth
channel_name: Developing country-based NGO
project_title: 2023 Agriculture-Focused Dignified Employment for Youth in Ethiopia (ADEY)

long_description: Arabuko Limited was founded in 2015 and incorporated as a limited company in 2018. Arabuko works directly with farmers as well as aggregators across the region. Our products include fresh fruits, vegetables, cereals, meats, chicken eggs, honey and flowers. Our target market includes institutions such as hospitals, schools, supermarkets, hotels, exporters, global markets and manufacturers.    Arabuko intends to implement a new project where we will enhance the aggregator model by focusing on empowering young women to become polished entrepreneurs through capacity building and linking them to markets for their products and aggregators in the agricultural sector. We also wish to work with farmers in encouraging them to embrace safe farming practices and provide market assurance for their produce.  We are targeting fifty groups led by the super aggregators and the farmers whom they get their products from.  The aggregator can also practice farming and can have the aggregation point in their farms.  We will group the aggregators into fifty groups. We will work in Kajiado, Makueni, Machakos, Nairobi and Kiambu counties based on the accessibility of the aggregators and the farmers. These are regions where we actively have aggregators and farmers that we support by providing a market for their products. These areas are also suitable for access to the main Nairobi markets, the export processing zones and the airport.    The aggregator enhancing project is one that focuses on young women aggregators by building capacity, creating a ready market and forming an association of women suppliers and exporters in twelve months which will help in the creation of employment opportunities, ensure gender and financial inclusion and the in long run help in promoting food security and in the process contribute in the eradication of poverty. The aggregators will be required to have a minimum of ten farmers preferably women that they work closely with. The aggregators will have to show us the farms and the farmers. Our focus will be on the vulnerable groups who have never received any financial training or entrepreneurial capacity-building interventions. Some of the focus training will be in entrepreneurship skills, agricultural training, standards and food safety, value addition and gender safeguarding policies. Some of our goals will be easy access to financial assistance through our partners, registration of formal business which will give them a competitive advantage in the market place and for them to be part of The Young Women Suppliers and Exporters Association and for the farmers in our program to have access to competitive markets and practice proper farming techniques through our training interventions.     Expected Outcomes,  Aggregators  1. Acquire life skills, learn about group dynamics and acquire leadership skills.  2. Adopt proper entrepreneurship skills, be financial, digital literate and have proper business plan.  3. Be knowledgeable in HACCP and ISO standards to ensure food safety for their target markets. Acquire the standard certificates.  4. Acquire value addition skills on their products hence obtain better markets for their products.  5. Trade at global levels. The farmers should be able to export their products through the association.  Farmers,  1. Farmers should have proper farm and financial records. Be financial and digital literate and market their products.  2. Farmers should adopt safe and best farming practices, get expert apiculture awareness and access agronomy support.  3. Farmers should acquire value addition skills and be aware of crops that Kenya exports and the accepted standards and how they can acquire the export market.

long_description: Overall rating is Medium. Aquaculture Barn Limited is aligned to the Foundations charitable objective on the Advancement of Education. Aquabarn project will adopt a Farmer Specialization Farming Model that will be providing training in tilapia breeding and eggs harvesting which will allow 700 young women and young men (70%young women) to generate a consistent income every 10-21 days. By training and supporting these target individuals in tilapia breeding and eggs harvesting, Aquaculture barn believes that they can create a network of successful small-scale tilapia breeders that will consistently supply the local tilapia hatcheries with adequate eggs, contributing to the local economy and improved livelihoods. There are no red flags noted (Organisation name, Board members and management team) on Dow Jones screening.     Aquaculture does not have child and youth protection and safeguarding policies. However, they are willing to adopt the Foundation's   child & youth and safeguarding policies.     In addition, Aquaculture requires capacity on financial management, project management, child & youth protection and safeguarding policies, market linkages and institutional strengthening.     The benefit will accrue to all the 700 young men and women who are below 35 years old of which 70% will be women.      Aquabarn project will adopt a Farmer Specialization Farming Model that will be providing training in tilapia breeding and eggs harvesting which will allow 700 young women and young men (70%young women) to generate a consistent income every 10-21 days.     Aquabarn project will focus on one intervention area : Training 500 young women and men in tilapia eggs production and marketing.     There were noted capacity needs which include financial management, institutional strengthening, project management, child& youth protection and safeguarding policies and these have been consolidated and a capacity plan Is underway for the support of YLOs.      The benefits that accrue from this partnership will all go to the 700 young men and women of which 70% will be young women.      In the spirit of boosting our current proportion of youth led organizations/ partnerships from 8% to 15%, I hereby recommend the Foundation to partner with this organization.     Key Objectives and Benefits     To transfer skills and knowledge in brood stock selection and spawning techniques that the young women and young men can replicate in their own farms and the communities to generate income.      To teach young women and men about fishpond preparation and management for tilapia breeding     To train the participants in tilapia eggs harvesting and handling techniques     To provide adequate knowledge in water quality management for breeding ponds     To link the participants with hatcheries where they can sell their tilapia eggs.     Core Activities     Identify and engage with the target communities through 7 demo sites each supporting 55-100 participants.       Set-up training materials for young women and men on brood stock management, tilapia egg harvesting, and business development.     Provide      Identify potential hatcheries as buyers of the eggs and facilitate linkages between participants and the hatcheries.       Expected Impact.     Enhanced capacity and skills of 700 young women and young men in tilapia breeding and eggs harvesting.       Amplify the voices of women and young men against cultural discrimination and segregation to promote meaningful participation in the aquaculture sector.      Young women and men who previously engaged in transactional sex for fish will have a diverse income stream in the production of tilapia fish eggs and, with the development of aquaculture, have ready access to fish that will not expose them to exploitation.     Increased revenues and improved livelihoods for the young women and men, their families and communities.      Increased the number of participants in the aquaculture sector by having more men and wom
